text:
The rapid expansion of multi-sensor technology and the proliferation of massive datasets have exposed the constraints of traditional matrix-based data models. This paper provides a comprehensive signal processing perspective on the transition from two-way to multiway component analysis using higher-order tensors. By leveraging the mathematical framework of multilinear algebra, tensor decompositions offer a fundamental paradigm shift toward models that are essentially polynomial in nature. Unlike matrix methods, these multidimensional models ensure uniqueness under mild conditions and provide superior flexibility in incorporating constraints that reflect underlying data properties, such as sparsity, independence, and nonnegativity.

The authors review the algebraic foundations of tensor modeling, navigating from basic Canonical Polyadic (CPD) and Tucker decompositions to sophisticated schemes for multi-view data analysis and block-term models. The discussion demonstrates how tensor frameworks naturally generalize core signal processing tasks, including canonical correlation, subspace techniques, signal separation, and feature extraction. To address the "curse of dimensionality" inherent in big data, the paper explores computational strategies such as tensor networks (e.g., Tensor Train) and compressed sensing for efficient storage and manipulation. Supported by real-world case studies in fields ranging from brain-computer interfaces to hyperspectral imaging, the paper highlights how "tensorization" can reveal latent structures in data that remain hidden to standard flat-view matrix methods.
